evals.prompt: Write CSS theme tokens that meet text contrast requirements in light and dark modes.
evals.expected_output: Defines color tokens with sufficient contrast and uses them through var() in both modes.
expectations: Normal text contrast is handled through theme tokens
Light and dark values are both present
Component styles reference vars instead of raw colors
The theme is designed for both modes

evals.prompt: Write CSS for a checkbox or button border that meets non-text contrast requirements.
evals.expected_output: Uses a border or other visible boundary that reaches 3:1 contrast instead of relying on a faint shadow.
expectations: The boundary is visible at 3:1 contrast
The control does not rely only on shadow
The control boundary is perceivable in both themes
The interactive element remains understandable

evals.prompt: Write focus styles that remain visible in light, dark, and forced-colors modes.
evals.expected_output: Uses :focus-visible with an outline-based focus ring and a forced-colors override.
expectations: Keyboard focus is clearly visible
The style uses :focus-visible
Forced-colors mode has an explicit fallback
Focus styling does not disappear on dark surfaces

evals.prompt: Refactor a status indicator so it does not depend on color alone.
evals.expected_output: Adds text, icons, or labels alongside color changes so the meaning is clear without color.
expectations: Color is not the only cue
Text or icon supports the status meaning
The state remains understandable to color-blind users
The status indicator is not purely visual color encoding

evals.prompt: Write CSS for links and UI components so contrast is preserved in both themes.
evals.expected_output: Uses a consistent token system and preserves enough contrast for text, icons, and focus indicators.
expectations: Links have sufficient contrast in both modes
Icons and UI controls also meet contrast requirements
Theme switching does not break contrast
Contrast is handled through reusable tokens
